[JS Weird World] No.60 Strict Mode: Make JavaScript Stop Covering for Your Typos

After the last few chapters, you've probably noticed something — JavaScript is a really loose, freewheeling language. But if you'd rather have it act stricter, that's totally fine too. There's just one little thing you add, and JavaScript flips into strict mode.

Honestly, switching to strict mode usually comes with a bit of a painful adjustment period. People get used to freedom, right? But the whole point of strict mode is to head off some unnecessary, weird problems. So how do you turn it on for your own JavaScript? And what changes once it's on? Let's look at an example.
Here's some code in non-strict mode. Typos are basically the most common thing we do as developers, so what happens with something like this? Does it throw an error?
language-js shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
Non-strict mode logs the object with no error
Nope. No error at all. Why? Because of JavaScript's loose nature — when the syntax parser hits
in the current execution context. If it doesn't find one, it just takes the liberty of adding a
for us. And that's exactly the kind of thing that leads to nasty debugging headaches.
So how do you avoid that? This is where strict mode comes in. Turning it on is dead simple — you just put
at the very top. Now let's see what the same example does.
Strict mode throws a reference error for the typo
This time we get "Persom is not defined, uncaught reference error".
One detail to watch out for:
has to go at the very top of all your code, otherwise it has no effect.
Strict mode placed at the bottom has no effect
You can also scope strict mode to a specific execution context.
Strict mode scoped inside a function
is a great way to cut down on unnecessary errors. That said, I'd usually recommend pairing it with some JavaScript style management (ESLint), or rewriting things in TypeScript.
I haven't learned TypeScript myself yet, so I can't give you a hands-on example here, but I'll drop some reference material below.

After the last few chapters, you've probably noticed how loose JavaScript can be. But if you want it to be stricter, that's totally doable. There's a single line you can add to flip JavaScript into strict mode — and it'll stop quietly covering for the kind of typos that cause nasty bugs.
